title: On This Nearly Deserted Alaska Island, You Pay to Keep Hundreds of Empty Buildings Wired for Internet
excerpt: U.S. phone customers pay more than $340,000 a year for an Anchorage company to keep homes and buildings on Alaska’s Adak Island wired for obsolete internet service. All but a few of them sit empty.
content_html:
On an island in the Bering Sea 1,200 miles from Anchorage, Alaska, sits an abandoned house on an abandoned Navy base. There is no furniture, and the wind blows through broken windows. The last people to live here moved away long ago. Yet a bright orange pipe with a black fiber-optic cable inside runs from the outer wall down into the surrounding soil.  

The cable was installed to connect the home to the internet after the base closed. It costs more than $340,000 per year to provide this building and hundreds of others — all but a few of them empty — with internet access no one says they use. 

The money being wasted is yours. 

It comes from a fee added to nearly every American’s phone bill as part of a Federal Communications Commission subsidy program intended to bring fast, affordable internet to remote towns.

Reporting this year by the Anchorage Daily News and ProPublica has found that the multibillion-dollar Universal Service Fund pays for what often amounts to slow internet access for customers who actually no longer use it. 

The FCC keeps the money flowing to telecom companies regardless of their owners’ track records. It kept paying a Fairbanks company to provide internet service while its owner served federal prison time for tax evasion. The government kept paying Alaska’s largest telecom, GCI, even after it signed a settlement for alleged fraud related to the subsidy. (The settlement said it was neither an admission of guilt by GCI nor a concession by the Justice Department that the claims were not well founded.)

Yet few places make the federal government’s failure to safeguard this money more obvious than Adak. 

Once home to thousands of sailors and their families, the island emptied after the base closed in 1997. While the state estimated the population to be 77 last year, residents themselves put it at closer to two dozen — meaning the federal internet subsidy to the local telecom amounts to about $14,000 a year per person. And, it turns out, there’s zero indication any of these people are benefiting from it. 

We know this because we flew to the island in June and visited every building the company itself told regulators it serves. We knocked on every door. Talked to every person we could find. 

Not a single one told us they subscribe to the island’s subsidized internet service.

Residents get online through Starlink, the Elon Musk satellite service that arrived in 2023 without any subsidy from the FCC. People here say it’s up to 40 times faster and costs $90 to $140 per month, compared with the hundreds of dollars a month that Adak Eagle, the local telecom, once collected from customers on top of its subsidy checks. (Adak Eagle’s website currently advertises internet plans for $99 to $550 per month.)

But the subsidies remain.

Like many Alaska telecoms, Adak Eagle was built on public money. It began in the early 2000s with FCC subsidies and federal loans to upgrade old telephone wires for fiber-optic lines that could support both voice and internet service. By 2011, the company was collecting $2.68 million a year from the Universal Service Fund.

A 2013 federal order determined company president Larry Mayes spent telecom subsidy money on a fleet of vehicles and a fishing boat that weren’t necessary for his business, and that the $237,455 salary Mayes paid himself one year was “unreasonable” and “disproportionate” given the size of the company and what’s typical for Anchorage, where he lives. 

(The company filed letters with the FCC at the time stating its salaries “should be evaluated in the context of a small company where each employee wears multiple hats” and citing the island’s “remoteness and harsh climate conditions.”) 

On top of the $3.6 million his company has received from the federal broadband subsidy since 2016, he also received $3.54 million from the FCC through a program that replaces revenue phone companies once earned connecting long-distance calls. The state kicked in an additional $3.75 million through its own subsidy program, the Alaska Universal Service Fund. (Some Adak residents said they continue to pay the company for landlines that they use as cellphone backup.)

When we first asked Mayes about the federal internet money, he hung up on us. He did not respond to questions emailed in May, a written interview request delivered to his Adak office in June or detailed questions emailed and hand-delivered to his Anchorage office in July. Reached again by phone on July 13, he declined to comment and ended the call.  

The one thing Mayes did say before hanging up on the first call: that he is playing by the rules of the FCC’s subsidy program, and if we had questions about it, we should ask the agency. 

We did. Repeatedly. About the publicly funded connections to demolished homes on Adak and other uses of internet subsidies in Alaska.

The FCC never responded.

Internet for Vacant Homes

Step outside Adak’s tiny airport terminal, and the scene is straight out of “The Walking Dead.” You can spend hours walking the city streets without seeing another human, the only sounds the dry scrape of tall grass and warbling seabirds. Stiffened copies of People magazine declare George Clooney the 2006 Sexiest Man Alive. A shuttered McDonald’s drive-thru still advertises 99-cent hamburgers. 

Subdivisions outside the city core sprawl like any in America, with neatly arranged ranch-style homes, powder-blue roofs, wide driveways and generous garages. After the Navy shipped out, hurricane-force Aleutian winds pried homes apart. The worst of it is in a beachfront neighborhood called “Officer’s Country” on old city maps. Bathroom mirrors and toilets and kitchen tables stand exposed to the rain in homes cleaved in half like dollhouses. 

Even in newer neighborhoods, we found rotted exterior walls with holes so wide you could drive a truck inside. Grass grew on living room floors. Bedrooms were filled with bird nests.

In the few homes the FCC paid Adak Eagle to connect that are habitable, the occupants have abandoned Adak Eagle.  

Henry Collins, 61, lives in one of those homes. He was partially blinded in a chemical accident years ago while cleaning a boat at the island docks. Now he and his wife spend their days caring for their newborn. Sometimes they FaceTime with his wife’s family in the Philippines and hold the baby up to the screen.

He accidentally cut Adak Eagle’s internet cable with a string trimmer. No matter. He never could afford the subsidized service anyway, and his rooftop Starlink receiver was untouched in the landscaping incident. 

One street over, 26-year-old Steven Ivanoff pays close attention to internet speeds because he plays Overwatch and Fortnite competitively online.

Before Starlink, residents said, Ivanoff’s former roommate and fellow gamer was notorious for using up the scant bandwidth Adak Eagle provided every time he went online to play. Neighbors hollered at him to get off the damn computer. Adak Eagle’s subsidized fiber offers download speeds of 1 to 10 megabits per second, according to the FCC National Broadband Map — far short of the FCC’s 100-megabit standard for broadband and, at the low end, barely fast enough to load a modern webpage. 

All that has changed. At our request, Ivanoff tested the speed of his Starlink connection using an online service called speedtest.net. He clocked it at a screaming (by Alaska standards) 447 megabits per second.

At another house, four dachshunds stood guard in a fenced yard while inside, an agent with the Transportation Security Administration who moved from Arkansas was playing a reality TV show on her widescreen — Starlink-powered, like everyone else’s internet.

Elizabeth Pryor has leaned into the isolation by starting a mini farm. In her solarium, a cardboard box trembled with teenage chicks, peeping under a heat lamp. No bears on the island, she said. So that’s nice. No foxes either. “But the rats ate my jalapeno peppers down to the stalk! Chomp chomp chomp.”

Her house rises across the street from a pile of rubble, the remains of a house fire. 

Both properties — the rubble pile and Pryor’s home, alive with puppies and chickens — are on the list of houses for which Adak Eagle received subsidies to connect to the internet. She doesn’t use it. 

Wiring an Empty Island

Adak was already a ghost town when Mayes’ company started wiring the island.

According to the U.S. Navy’s own Record of Decision on the base closure, published in 2000, “there was no established community on Adak” after the military’s departure. The Navy’s ongoing assessments documented houses being condemned and boarded up. 

Andilea Weaver, the company’s former vice president, said Adak Eagle began replacing rat-gnawed copper landline wires to Adak buildings in 2006 — nine years after the base closure. 

In 2016, Adak Eagle proposed a 10-year plan to receive new subsidies in exchange for connecting or maintaining lines to 346 locations, nearly every building left standing. The FCC agreed. The agency formally declared the new spending plan to be in the public interest.

The FCC in 2022 reduced the number of locations Adak Eagle was obligated to serve to 306 after the company reported some buildings were unlivable, but the agency left the subsidy amount unchanged. 

According to federal data published by the Universal Service Administrative Co., the most locations that Adak Eagle reported having broadband available was 283.

The buildings the FCC says Adak Eagle keeps wired for internet include duplexes, fourplexes and ranches where we found no sign of life. The agency shows connections at places where we found doors yawning open in the wind, hinges screaming with rust — no furniture inside, no appliances, yet in many cases, a single telephone still plugged into a wall.

Whether the abandoned structures Adak Eagle has prepped for low-speed internet can beckon new occupants is an open question.

Adak is so far west of the Alaska mainland that it’s in a different time zone. So far west that it’s almost in the Eastern Hemisphere.

The last vestige of industry departed with the closure of the island’s fish processing plant in 2020. Now the economy consists of a bit of hunting and bird-watching that lures a few hardy tourists aboard the two flights a week that serve the island. (Warning: Pack canned goods. The  grocery store and restaurant were closed our entire visit.)

Alaska’s Republican U.S. Sen. Dan Sullivan has argued the U.S. should open a new military base on Adak because of its strategic proximity to waters cruised by the Chinese and Russian navies. With $115 million set aside by Congress last year for “exploration and development of existing Arctic infrastructure,” a Pentagon-led task force is developing recommendations on how to proceed, a Sullivan spokesperson said.

After knocking on hundreds of doors and canvassing every subdivision, we spent our last hours in Adak checking to see if anyone was using the subsidized internet in the old commercial buildings and offices scattered around the center of the city. 

At one of the last addresses on the list crouched a cinder block building surrounded by pickup trucks and wide spools of orange piping — the same piping we’d seen leading up to demolished houses the day before. A rotary phone hung on the wall. 

This was the local office of the company we’d been examining. Even the people who work there have stopped relying on the internet service that you pay for. Above the front door, above the sign for Adak Eagle Enterprises, stood two Starlink dishes.

sentences.zh: 虽然该州去年估计人口为 77 人，但居民自己估计接近两打——这意味着联邦对当地电信公司的互联网补贴约为每人每年 14,000 美元。

sentences.en: And, it turns out, there’s zero indication any of these people are benefiting from it.
sentences.zh: 事实证明，没有任何迹象表明这些人从中受益。

blocks.html_en: We know this because we flew to the island in June and visited every building the company itself told regulators it serves. We knocked on every door. Talked to every person we could find.

sentences.en: We know this because we flew to the island in June and visited every building the company itself told regulators it serves.
sentences.zh: 我们之所以知道这一点，是因为我们于 6 月份飞往该岛，参观了该公司告诉监管机构其服务的每一栋建筑。

sentences.en: We knocked on every door.
sentences.zh: 我们敲了每一扇门。

sentences.en: Talked to every person we could find.
sentences.zh: 与我们能找到的每个人交谈。

blocks.html_en: Not a single one told us they subscribe to the island’s subsidized internet service.

sentences.en: Not a single one told us they subscribe to the island’s subsidized internet service.
sentences.zh: 没有人告诉我们他们订阅了岛上的补贴互联网服务。

blocks.html_en: Residents get online through Starlink, the Elon Musk satellite service that arrived in 2023 without any subsidy from the FCC. People here say it’s up to 40 times faster and costs $90 to $140 per month, compared with the hundreds of dollars a month that Adak Eagle, the local telecom, once collected from customers on top of its subsidy checks. (Adak Eagle’s website currently advertises internet plans for $99 to $550 per month.)

sentences.en: Residents get online through Starlink, the Elon Musk satellite service that arrived in 2023 without any subsidy from the FCC.
sentences.zh: 居民通过 Starlink 上网，这是埃隆·马斯克 (Elon Musk) 于 2023 年推出的卫星服务，没有获得 FCC 的任何补贴。

sentences.en: People here say it’s up to 40 times faster and costs $90 to $140 per month, compared with the hundreds of dollars a month that Adak Eagle, the local telecom, once collected from customers on top of its subsidy checks.
sentences.zh: 这里的人们说，它的速度提高了 40 倍，每月费用为 90 至 140 美元，而当地电信公司 Adak Eagle 每月除了补贴支票外还要向客户收取数百美元。

sentences.en: (Adak Eagle’s website currently advertises internet plans for $99 to $550 per month.)
sentences.zh: （Adak Eagle 的网站目前广告的互联网套餐价格为每月 99 至 550 美元。）

blocks.html_en: But the subsidies remain.

sentences.en: But the subsidies remain.
sentences.zh: 但补贴仍然存在。

blocks.html_en: Like many Alaska telecoms, Adak Eagle was built on public money. It began in the early 2000s with FCC subsidies and federal loans to upgrade old telephone wires for fiber-optic lines that could support both voice and internet service. By 2011, the company was collecting $2.68 million a year from the Universal Service Fund.

sentences.en: Like many Alaska telecoms, Adak Eagle was built on public money.
sentences.zh: 与许多阿拉斯加电信公司一样，Adak Eagle 也是靠公共资金建立的。

sentences.en: It began in the early 2000s with FCC subsidies and federal loans to upgrade old telephone wires for fiber-optic lines that could support both voice and internet service.
sentences.zh: 它始于 2000 年代初，利用 FCC 补贴和联邦贷款将旧电话线升级为光纤线路，以支持语音和互联网服务。

sentences.en: By 2011, the company was collecting $2.68 million a year from the Universal Service Fund.
sentences.zh: 到 2011 年，该公司每年从普遍服务基金筹集 268 万美元。

sentences.en: Once-occupied homes along Kuluk Bay have been ravaged by weather and time since Adak was an important Cold War outpost.
sentences.zh: 由于埃达克是冷战时期的一个重要前哨基地，库鲁克湾沿岸曾经有人居住的房屋已经受到天气和时间的破坏。

sentences.en: Marc Lester/ADN
sentences.zh: 马克·莱斯特/DNA

sentences.en: One of many play areas left in disrepair on residential streets in Adak Marc Lester/ADN
sentences.zh: Adak Marc Lester/ADN 住宅区街道上众多年久失修的游乐区之一

sentences.en: Once-occupied homes along Kuluk Bay have been ravaged by weather and time since Adak was an important Cold War outpost.
sentences.zh: 由于埃达克是冷战时期的一个重要前哨基地，库鲁克湾沿岸曾经有人居住的房屋已经受到天气和时间的破坏。

sentences.en: Marc Lester/ADN
sentences.zh: 马克·莱斯特/DNA

sentences.en: One of many play areas left in disrepair on residential streets in Adak Marc Lester/ADN
sentences.zh: Adak Marc Lester/ADN 住宅区街道上众多年久失修的游乐区之一

blocks.html_en: A 2013 federal order determined company president Larry Mayes spent telecom subsidy money on a fleet of vehicles and a fishing boat that weren’t necessary for his business, and that the $237,455 salary Mayes paid himself one year was “unreasonable” and “disproportionate” given the size of the company and what’s typical for Anchorage, where he lives.

sentences.en: A 2013 federal order determined company president Larry Mayes spent telecom subsidy money on a fleet of vehicles and a fishing boat that weren’t necessary for his business, and that the $237,455 salary Mayes paid himself one year was “unreasonable” and “disproportionate” given the size of the company and what’s typical for Anchorage, where he lives.
sentences.zh: 2013 年的一项联邦命令规定，公司总裁拉里·梅斯 (Larry Mayes) 将电信补贴资金用于购买其业务不需要的车队和渔船，而且考虑到公司的规模以及他所居住的安克雷奇的典型情况，梅斯一年为自己支付的 237,455 美元的工资是“不合理”且“不成比例”的。

blocks.html_en: (The company filed letters with the FCC at the time stating its salaries “should be evaluated in the context of a small company where each employee wears multiple hats” and citing the island’s “remoteness and harsh climate conditions.”)

sentences.en: (The company filed letters with the FCC at the time stating its salaries “should be evaluated in the context of a small company where each employee wears multiple hats” and citing the island’s “remoteness and harsh climate conditions.”)
sentences.zh: （该公司当时向联邦通信委员会提交了信函，表示其薪资“应该在小公司的背景下进行评估，每个员工都身兼数职”，并指出该岛“地处偏远，气候条件恶劣”。）

blocks.html_en: On top of the $3.6 million his company has received from the federal broadband subsidy since 2016, he also received $3.54 million from the FCC through a program that replaces revenue phone companies once earned connecting long-distance calls. The state kicked in an additional $3.75 million through its own subsidy program, the Alaska Universal Service Fund. (Some Adak residents said they continue to pay the company for landlines that they use as cellphone backup.)

sentences.en: On top of the $3.6 million his company has received from the federal broadband subsidy since 2016, he also received $3.54 million from the FCC through a program that replaces revenue phone companies once earned connecting long-distance calls.
sentences.zh: 自 2016 年以来，他的公司除了从联邦宽带补贴中获得了 360 万美元之外，还通过一项取代电话公司曾经通过连接长途电话赚取收入的计划，从 FCC 获得了 354 万美元。

sentences.en: The state kicked in an additional $3.75 million through its own subsidy program, the Alaska Universal Service Fund.
sentences.zh: 该州通过自己的补贴计划“阿拉斯加普遍服务基金”额外投入了 375 万美元。

sentences.en: (Some Adak residents said they continue to pay the company for landlines that they use as cellphone backup.)
sentences.zh: （一些埃达克居民表示，他们继续向该公司支付固定电话费用，并将其用作手机备份。）

blocks.html_en: When we first asked Mayes about the federal internet money, he hung up on us. He did not respond to questions emailed in May, a written interview request delivered to his Adak office in June or detailed questions emailed and hand-delivered to his Anchorage office in July. Reached again by phone on July 13, he declined to comment and ended the call.

sentences.en: When we first asked Mayes about the federal internet money, he hung up on us.
sentences.zh: 当我们第一次向梅斯询问联邦互联网资金的情况时，他挂断了我们的电话。

sentences.en: He did not respond to questions emailed in May, a written interview request delivered to his Adak office in June or detailed questions emailed and hand-delivered to his Anchorage office in July.
sentences.zh: 他没有回复 5 月份通过电子邮件发送的问题、6 月份发送到他的埃达克办公室的书面采访请求或 7 月份通过电子邮件发送并亲手递送到他的安克雷奇办公室的详细问题。

sentences.en: Reached again by phone on July 13, he declined to comment and ended the call.
sentences.zh: 7 月 13 日再次致电他时，他拒绝发表评论并结束了通话。

blocks.html_en: The one thing Mayes did say before hanging up on the first call: that he is playing by the rules of the FCC’s subsidy program, and if we had questions about it, we should ask the agency.

sentences.en: The one thing Mayes did say before hanging up on the first call: that he is playing by the rules of the FCC’s subsidy program, and if we had questions about it, we should ask the agency.
sentences.zh: 梅耶斯在挂断第一次电话之前说过的一件事是：他正在遵守 FCC 补贴计划的规则，如果我们对此有疑问，我们应该询问该机构。

blocks.html_en: We did. Repeatedly. About the publicly funded connections to demolished homes on Adak and other uses of internet subsidies in Alaska.

sentences.en: We did.
sentences.zh: 我们做到了。

sentences.en: Repeatedly.
sentences.zh: 反复。

sentences.en: About the publicly funded connections to demolished homes on Adak and other uses of internet subsidies in Alaska.
sentences.zh: 关于公共资助的与埃达克被拆除房屋的连接以及阿拉斯加互联网补贴的其他用途。

blocks.html_en: The FCC never responded.

sentences.en: The FCC never responded.
sentences.zh: FCC从未回应。

sentences.en: Internet for Vacant Homes
sentences.zh: 空置房屋互联网

sentences.zh: 马克·莱斯特/DNA

sentences.en: A vehicle kicks up dust on Bayshore Highway outside of the town of Adak on Adak Island.
sentences.zh: 一辆汽车在埃达克岛埃达克镇外的海湾高速公路上扬起灰尘。

sentences.zh: 马克·莱斯特/DNA

sentences.zh: 马克·莱斯特/DNA

blocks.html_en: Step outside Adak’s tiny airport terminal, and the scene is straight out of “The Walking Dead.” You can spend hours walking the city streets without seeing another human, the only sounds the dry scrape of tall grass and warbling seabirds. Stiffened copies of People magazine declare George Clooney the 2006 Sexiest Man Alive. A shuttered McDonald’s drive-thru still advertises 99-cent hamburgers.

sentences.en: Step outside Adak’s tiny airport terminal, and the scene is straight out of “The Walking Dead.”
sentences.zh: 走出埃达克的小型机场航站楼，眼前的场景就像是《行尸走肉》中的场景。

sentences.en: You can spend hours walking the city streets without seeing another human, the only sounds the dry scrape of tall grass and warbling seabirds.
sentences.zh: 你可以在城市街道上行走几个小时，却看不到另一个人，唯一的声音就是高高的草丛的干燥摩擦声和海鸟的鸣叫声。

sentences.en: Stiffened copies of People magazine declare George Clooney the 2006 Sexiest Man Alive.
sentences.zh: 《人物》杂志的硬拷贝宣称乔治·克鲁尼为 2006 年最性感男人。

sentences.en: A shuttered McDonald’s drive-thru still advertises 99-cent hamburgers.
sentences.zh: 一家已关门的麦当劳得来速餐厅仍在宣传 99 美分的汉堡包。

blocks.html_en: Subdivisions outside the city core sprawl like any in America, with neatly arranged ranch-style homes, powder-blue roofs, wide driveways and generous garages. After the Navy shipped out, hurricane-force Aleutian winds pried homes apart. The worst of it is in a beachfront neighborhood called “Officer’s Country” on old city maps. Bathroom mirrors and toilets and kitchen tables stand exposed to the rain in homes cleaved in half like dollhouses.

sentences.en: Subdivisions outside the city core sprawl like any in America, with neatly arranged ranch-style homes, powder-blue roofs, wide driveways and generous garages.
sentences.zh: 市中心以外的小区像美国的任何小区一样蔓延，拥有排列整齐的牧场式住宅、粉蓝色的屋顶、宽阔的车道和宽敞的车库。

sentences.en: After the Navy shipped out, hurricane-force Aleutian winds pried homes apart.
sentences.zh: 海军出发后，阿留申群岛的飓风将房屋吹得四分五裂。

sentences.en: The worst of it is in a beachfront neighborhood called “Officer’s Country” on old city maps.
sentences.zh: 最糟糕的是在旧城市地图上称为“军官之国”的海滨社区。

sentences.en: Bathroom mirrors and toilets and kitchen tables stand exposed to the rain in homes cleaved in half like dollhouses.
sentences.zh: 浴室的镜子、厕所和厨房的桌子暴露在雨中，房屋被分成两半，就像玩具屋一样。

blocks.html_en: Even in newer neighborhoods, we found rotted exterior walls with holes so wide you could drive a truck inside. Grass grew on living room floors. Bedrooms were filled with bird nests.

sentences.en: Even in newer neighborhoods, we found rotted exterior walls with holes so wide you could drive a truck inside.
sentences.zh: 即使在较新的社区，我们也发现腐烂的外墙，洞大到可以在里面开卡车。

sentences.en: Grass grew on living room floors.
sentences.zh: 客厅地板上长满了草。

sentences.en: Bedrooms were filled with bird nests.
sentences.zh: 卧室里堆满了鸟巢。

sentences.en: Exterior walls are missing on one home in a central subdivision of Adak.
sentences.zh: 埃达克中心分区的一栋房屋外墙缺失。

sentences.en: Marc Lester/ADN
sentences.zh: 马克·莱斯特/DNA

blocks.html_en: In the few homes the FCC paid Adak Eagle to connect that are habitable, the occupants have abandoned Adak Eagle.

sentences.en: In the few homes the FCC paid Adak Eagle to connect that are habitable, the occupants have abandoned Adak Eagle.
sentences.zh: 在美国联邦通信委员会 (FCC) 向埃达克伊格尔 (Adak Eagle) 付费连接的少数几处可居住的房屋中，居住者已经放弃了埃达克伊格尔 (Adak Eagle)。

blocks.html_en: Henry Collins, 61, lives in one of those homes. He was partially blinded in a chemical accident years ago while cleaning a boat at the island docks. Now he and his wife spend their days caring for their newborn. Sometimes they FaceTime with his wife’s family in the Philippines and hold the baby up to the screen.

sentences.en: Henry Collins, 61, lives in one of those homes.
sentences.zh: 61 岁的亨利·柯林斯 (Henry Collins) 就住在其中一所房子里。

sentences.en: He was partially blinded in a chemical accident years ago while cleaning a boat at the island docks.
sentences.zh: 几年前，他在岛上码头清洗一艘船时发生化学事故，导致部分失明。

sentences.en: Now he and his wife spend their days caring for their newborn.
sentences.zh: 现在，他和妻子每天都在照顾新生儿。

sentences.en: Sometimes they FaceTime with his wife’s family in the Philippines and hold the baby up to the screen.
sentences.zh: 有时他们会与菲律宾妻子的家人进行视频通话，并将婴儿抱到屏幕前。

blocks.html_en: He accidentally cut Adak Eagle’s internet cable with a string trimmer. No matter. He never could afford the subsidized service anyway, and his rooftop Starlink receiver was untouched in the landscaping incident.

sentences.en: He accidentally cut Adak Eagle’s internet cable with a string trimmer.
sentences.zh: 他不小心用剪线器剪断了埃达克伊格尔的网线。

sentences.en: No matter.
sentences.zh: 不管。

sentences.en: He never could afford the subsidized service anyway, and his rooftop Starlink receiver was untouched in the landscaping incident.
sentences.zh: 无论如何，他从来都负担不起这项补贴服务，而且他屋顶的星链接收器在景观美化事件中也没有受到影响。

blocks.html_en: One street over, 26-year-old Steven Ivanoff pays close attention to internet speeds because he plays Overwatch and Fortnite competitively online.

sentences.en: One street over, 26-year-old Steven Ivanoff pays close attention to internet speeds because he plays Overwatch and Fortnite competitively online.
sentences.zh: 一街之隔，26 岁的史蒂文·伊万诺夫 (Steven Ivanoff) 密切关注网速，因为他在网上玩《守望先锋》和《堡垒之夜》。

sentences.en: Steven Ivanoff, a year-round Adak resident, relies on Starlink to play video games with other people online.
sentences.zh: 史蒂文·伊万诺夫 (Steven Ivanoff) 常年居住在埃达克 (Adak)，他依靠星链与其他人在线玩视频游戏。

sentences.en: Marc Lester/ADN
sentences.zh: 马克·莱斯特/DNA

blocks.html_en: Before Starlink, residents said, Ivanoff’s former roommate and fellow gamer was notorious for using up the scant bandwidth Adak Eagle provided every time he went online to play. Neighbors hollered at him to get off the damn computer. Adak Eagle’s subsidized fiber offers download speeds of 1 to 10 megabits per second, according to the FCC National Broadband Map — far short of the FCC’s 100-megabit standard for broadband and, at the low end, barely fast enough to load a modern webpage.

sentences.en: Before Starlink, residents said, Ivanoff’s former roommate and fellow gamer was notorious for using up the scant bandwidth Adak Eagle provided every time he went online to play.
sentences.zh: 居民们表示，在 Starlink 出现之前，伊万诺夫的前室友兼游戏伙伴因每次上网玩游戏时都会占用 Adak Eagle 提供的有限带宽而臭名昭著。

sentences.en: Neighbors hollered at him to get off the damn computer.
sentences.zh: 邻居们对他大喊，让他离开那台该死的电脑。

sentences.en: Adak Eagle’s subsidized fiber offers download speeds of 1 to 10 megabits per second, according to the FCC National Broadband Map — far short of the FCC’s 100-megabit standard for broadband and, at the low end, barely fast enough to load a modern webpage.
sentences.zh: 根据 FCC 国家宽带地图，Adak Eagle 的补贴光纤提供每秒 1 到 10 兆比特的下载速度，远低于 FCC 的 100 兆宽带标准，而且在低端，速度勉强足以加载现代网页。

blocks.html_en: All that has changed. At our request, Ivanoff tested the speed of his Starlink connection using an online service called speedtest.net. He clocked it at a screaming (by Alaska standards) 447 megabits per second.

sentences.en: All that has changed.
sentences.zh: 一切都变了。

sentences.en: At our request, Ivanoff tested the speed of his Starlink connection using an online service called speedtest.net.
sentences.zh: 应我们的要求，伊万诺夫使用名为 speedtest.net 的在线服务测试了他的 Starlink 连接速度。

sentences.en: He clocked it at a screaming (by Alaska standards) 447 megabits per second.
sentences.zh: 他测得的速度高达每秒 447 兆比特（按照阿拉斯加标准）。

blocks.html_en: At another house, four dachshunds stood guard in a fenced yard while inside, an agent with the Transportation Security Administration who moved from Arkansas was playing a reality TV show on her widescreen — Starlink-powered, like everyone else’s internet.

sentences.en: At another house, four dachshunds stood guard in a fenced yard while inside, an agent with the Transportation Security Administration who moved from Arkansas was playing a reality TV show on her widescreen — Starlink-powered, like everyone else’s internet.
sentences.zh: 在另一所房子里，四只腊肠犬在栅栏院子里守卫，而在里面，一名从阿肯色州搬来的运输安全管理局特工正在她的宽屏上播放真人秀电视节目——由星链供电，就像其他人的互联网一样。

sentences.en: Elizabeth Pryor’s Adak home is on the list of those federally subsidized for internet service.
sentences.zh: 伊丽莎白·普赖尔 (Elizabeth Pryor) 的阿达克之家 (Elizabeth Pryor) 被列入联邦互联网服务补贴名单之列。

sentences.en: She uses Starlink for internet service, the standard for the town’s few remaining residents.
sentences.zh: 她使用星链提供互联网服务，这是该镇仅存的少数居民的标准。

sentences.en: Marc Lester/ADN
sentences.zh: 马克·莱斯特/DNA

sentences.en: Henry Collins said he accidentally cut the cord for Adak Eagle internet service, which he didn’t subscribe to anyway.
sentences.zh: 亨利·柯林斯 (Henry Collins) 表示，他不小心切断了 Adak Eagle 互联网服务的电源线，但他并没有订阅该服务。

sentences.en: He relies on Starlink instead.
sentences.zh: 他转而依赖星链。

sentences.en: Marc Lester/ADN
sentences.zh: 马克·莱斯特/DNA

blocks.html_en: Elizabeth Pryor has leaned into the isolation by starting a mini farm. In her solarium, a cardboard box trembled with teenage chicks, peeping under a heat lamp. No bears on the island, she said. So that’s nice. No foxes either. “But the rats ate my jalapeno peppers down to the stalk! Chomp chomp chomp.”

sentences.en: Elizabeth Pryor has leaned into the isolation by starting a mini farm.
sentences.zh: 伊丽莎白·普赖尔 (Elizabeth Pryor) 开始与世隔绝，创办了一个迷你农场。

sentences.en: In her solarium, a cardboard box trembled with teenage chicks, peeping under a heat lamp.
sentences.zh: 在她的日光浴室里，一个纸板箱里装着十几岁的小鸡，它们在热灯下偷看。

sentences.en: No bears on the island, she said.
sentences.zh: 她说，岛上没有熊。

sentences.en: So that’s nice.
sentences.zh: 所以这很好。

sentences.en: No foxes either.

content_html_zh:
在距阿拉斯加安克雷奇 1,200 英里的白令海岛屿上，一座废弃的海军基地内坐落着一座废弃的房屋。 没有家具，风从破碎的窗户吹进来。 最后居住在这里的人很久以前就搬走了。 然而，一根亮橙色的管道，里面有一根黑色的光缆，从外壁一直延伸到周围的土壤中。

基地关闭后，安装了电缆以将家庭连接到互联网。 每年花费超过 34 万美元为这栋建筑和其他数百栋建筑（除了少数几栋空着之外）提供互联网接入，但没有人说他们使用过。

被浪费的钱是你的。

它来自几乎每个美国人的电话账单中添加的一项费用，作为联邦通信委员会补贴计划的一部分，该计划旨在为偏远城镇提供快速、负担得起的互联网。

《安克雷奇日报》和 ProPublica 今年的报道发现，价值数十亿美元的普遍服务基金为实际上不再使用互联网的客户支付了通常会减慢互联网访问速度的费用。

无论电信公司所有者的记录如何，联邦通信委员会都会让资金流向电信公司。 它继续向费尔班克斯一家公司付费以提供互联网服务，而其所有者因逃税而在联邦监狱服刑。 即使在签署了与补贴相关的涉嫌欺诈的和解协议后，政府仍继续向阿拉斯加最大的电信公司 GCI 付款。 （和解协议称，这既不是 GCI 认罪，也不是司法部承认这些指控没有充分根据）。

然而，很少有地方比埃达克更能体现出联邦政府在保护这笔资金方面的失败。

该岛曾经是数千名水手及其家人的家园，但在 1997 年基地关闭后就空无一人。 虽然该州去年估计人口为 77 人，但居民自己估计接近两打——这意味着联邦对当地电信公司的互联网补贴约为每人每年 14,000 美元。 事实证明，没有任何迹象表明这些人从中受益。

我们之所以知道这一点，是因为我们于 6 月份飞往该岛，参观了该公司告诉监管机构其服务的每一栋建筑。 我们敲了每一扇门。 与我们能找到的每个人交谈。

没有人告诉我们他们订阅了岛上的补贴互联网服务。

居民通过 Starlink 上网，这是埃隆·马斯克 (Elon Musk) 于 2023 年推出的卫星服务，没有获得 FCC 的任何补贴。 这里的人们说，它的速度提高了 40 倍，每月费用为 90 至 140 美元，而当地电信公司 Adak Eagle 每月除了补贴支票外还要向客户收取数百美元。 （Adak Eagle 的网站目前广告的互联网套餐价格为每月 99 至 550 美元。）

但补贴仍然存在。

与许多阿拉斯加电信公司一样，Adak Eagle 也是靠公共资金建立的。 它始于 2000 年代初，利用 FCC 补贴和联邦贷款将旧电话线升级为光纤线路，以支持语音和互联网服务。 到 2011 年，该公司每年从普遍服务基金筹集 268 万美元。

2013 年的一项联邦命令规定，公司总裁拉里·梅斯 (Larry Mayes) 将电信补贴资金用于购买其业务不需要的车队和渔船，而且考虑到公司的规模以及他所居住的安克雷奇的典型情况，梅斯一年为自己支付的 237,455 美元的工资是“不合理”且“不成比例”的。

（该公司当时向联邦通信委员会提交了信函，表示其薪资“应该在小公司的背景下进行评估，每个员工都身兼数职”，并指出该岛“地处偏远，气候条件恶劣”。）

自 2016 年以来，他的公司除了从联邦宽带补贴中获得了 360 万美元之外，还通过一项取代电话公司曾经通过连接长途电话赚取收入的计划，从 FCC 获得了 354 万美元。 该州通过自己的补贴计划“阿拉斯加普遍服务基金”额外投入了 375 万美元。 （一些埃达克居民表示，他们继续向该公司支付固定电话费用，并将其用作手机备份。）

当我们第一次向梅斯询问联邦互联网资金的情况时，他挂断了我们的电话。 他没有回复 5 月份通过电子邮件发送的问题、6 月份发送到他的埃达克办公室的书面采访请求或 7 月份通过电子邮件发送并亲手递送到他的安克雷奇办公室的详细问题。 7 月 13 日再次致电他时，他拒绝发表评论并结束了通话。

梅耶斯在挂断第一次电话之前说过的一件事是：他正在遵守 FCC 补贴计划的规则，如果我们对此有疑问，我们应该询问该机构。

我们做到了。 反复。 关于公共资助的与埃达克被拆除房屋的连接以及阿拉斯加互联网补贴的其他用途。

FCC从未回应。

空置房屋互联网

走出埃达克的小型机场航站楼，眼前的场景就像是《行尸走肉》中的场景。 你可以在城市街道上行走几个小时，却看不到另一个人，唯一的声音就是高高的草丛的干燥摩擦声和海鸟的鸣叫声。 《人物》杂志的硬拷贝宣称乔治·克鲁尼为 2006 年最性感男人。 一家已关门的麦当劳得来速餐厅仍在宣传 99 美分的汉堡包。

市中心以外的小区像美国的任何小区一样蔓延，拥有排列整齐的牧场式住宅、粉蓝色的屋顶、宽阔的车道和宽敞的车库。 海军出发后，阿留申群岛的飓风将房屋吹得四分五裂。 最糟糕的是在旧城市地图上称为“军官之国”的海滨社区。 浴室的镜子、厕所和厨房的桌子暴露在雨中，房屋被分成两半，就像玩具屋一样。

即使在较新的社区，我们也发现腐烂的外墙，洞大到可以在里面开卡车。 客厅地板上长满了草。 卧室里堆满了鸟巢。

在美国联邦通信委员会 (FCC) 向埃达克伊格尔 (Adak Eagle) 付费连接的少数几处可居住的房屋中，居住者已经放弃了埃达克伊格尔 (Adak Eagle)。

61 岁的亨利·柯林斯 (Henry Collins) 就住在其中一所房子里。 几年前，他在岛上码头清洗一艘船时发生化学事故，导致部分失明。 现在，他和妻子每天都在照顾新生儿。 有时他们会与菲律宾妻子的家人进行视频通话，并将婴儿抱到屏幕前。

他不小心用剪线器剪断了埃达克伊格尔的网线。 不管。 无论如何，他从来都负担不起这项补贴服务，而且他屋顶的星链接收器在景观美化事件中也没有受到影响。

一街之隔，26 岁的史蒂文·伊万诺夫 (Steven Ivanoff) 密切关注网速，因为他在网上玩《守望先锋》和《堡垒之夜》。

居民们表示，在 Starlink 出现之前，伊万诺夫的前室友兼游戏伙伴因每次上网玩游戏时都会占用 Adak Eagle 提供的有限带宽而臭名昭著。 邻居们对他大喊，让他离开那台该死的电脑。 根据 FCC 国家宽带地图，Adak Eagle 的补贴光纤提供每秒 1 到 10 兆比特的下载速度，远低于 FCC 的 100 兆宽带标准，而且在低端，速度勉强足以加载现代网页。

一切都变了。 应我们的要求，伊万诺夫使用名为 speedtest.net 的在线服务测试了他的 Starlink 连接速度。 他测得的速度高达每秒 447 兆比特（按照阿拉斯加标准）。

在另一所房子里，四只腊肠犬在栅栏院子里守卫，而在里面，一名从阿肯色州搬来的运输安全管理局特工正在她的宽屏上播放真人秀电视节目——由星链供电，就像其他人的互联网一样。

伊丽莎白·普赖尔 (Elizabeth Pryor) 开始与世隔绝，创办了一个迷你农场。 在她的日光浴室里，一个纸板箱里装着十几岁的小鸡，它们在热灯下偷看。 她说，岛上没有熊。 所以这很好。 也没有狐狸。 “但是老鼠把我的墨西哥辣椒吃到了茎！ 咳咳咳。”

她的房子在街对面的一堆瓦砾中拔地而起，那是一场房屋火灾的废墟。

这两处房产——瓦砾堆和普赖尔的家，里面养着小狗和小鸡——都在埃达克伊格尔获得连接互联网补贴的房屋名单上。 她不使用它。

空岛布线

当梅耶斯的公司开始为岛上布线时，埃达克已经成了一座鬼城。

根据美国海军 2000 年发布的关于关闭基地的决定记录，军队撤离后“埃达克上没有建立社区”。 海军正在进行的评估记录了房屋被毁坏并用木板封起来的情况。

该公司前副总裁安迪莉亚·韦弗 (Andilea Weaver) 表示，Adak Eagle 于 2006 年（基地关闭九年后）开始更换埃达克大楼内被老鼠啃咬的固定铜线。

2016 年，Adak Eagle 提出了一项 10 年计划，通过连接或维护 346 个地点的线路（几乎所有建筑物都完好无损）来获得新的补贴。 美国联邦通信委员会同意了。 该机构正式宣布新的支出计划符合公共利益。

2022 年，在 Adak Eagle 报告一些建筑物不适合居住后，FCC 将其有义务提供服务的地点数量减少到 306 个，但该机构维持补贴金额不变。

根据普遍服务管理公司公布的联邦数据，Adak Eagle 报告提供宽带服务的地点最多为 283 个。

联邦通信委员会称，埃达克伊格尔保持互联网连接的建筑物包括复式公寓、四层公寓和牧场，我们在这些建筑中没有发现生命迹象。 该机构在我们发现门在风中张开、铰链因生锈而尖叫的地方展示了连接——里面没有家具，没有电器，但在许多情况下，一部电话仍然插在墙上。

Adak Eagle 为低速互联网准备的废弃建筑是否可以吸引新的居住者，这是一个悬而未决的问题。

埃达克位于阿拉斯加大陆以西很远的地方，处于不同的时区。 向西那么远，几乎在东半球。

随着 2020 年岛上鱼类加工厂的关闭，最后的工业遗迹消失了。 现在，这里的经济主要由狩猎和观鸟组成，这吸引了一些顽强的游客乘坐每周两班服务该岛的航班。 （警告：包装罐头食品。 我们整个访问过程中杂货店和餐厅都关门了。）

阿拉斯加州共和党美国参议员 丹·沙利文认为，美国应该在埃达克建立一个新的军事基地，因为该基地战略上靠近中国和俄罗斯海军巡航的水域。 沙利文发言人表示，国会去年拨出 1.15 亿美元用于“勘探和开发现有北极基础设施”，五角大楼领导的特别工作组正在制定如何开展的建议。

在敲开了数百扇门并走访了每个分区之后，我们在埃达克度过了最后几个小时，检查是否有人在散落在市中心的旧商业建筑和办公室中使用补贴互联网。

在名单上的最后一个地址中，坐落着一座煤渣砌块建筑，周围是皮卡车和橙色管道的宽线轴——与我们前一天看到的通向被拆除房屋的管道相同。 墙上挂着一部旋转电话。

这是我们正在检查的公司的当地办事处。 即使是在那里工作的人也不再依赖你付费的互联网服务。 前门上方，埃达克鹰企业的招牌上方，矗立着两个星链天线。
